facts_disclosed: Presenting at the Gulf Sports Medicine Conference on July 12, 2026
Topic: return-to-sport protocols after ACL reconstruction
Proud and nervous about it, needs to prepare

turns.content: I NEED TO TELL YOU SOMETHING. I got an email this morning and I literally screamed in my apartment. My neighbor probably thinks I'm losing it haha!!
That sounds like very exciting news! What happened?

They accepted my abstract!! For the Gulf Sports Medicine Conference!! I submitted it like two months ago and honestly I thought there was no way because the conference is HUGE — all the top sports medicine doctors and PTs in the region present there. But they said yes, gente. They actually said yes!
Congratulations — that's a huge accomplishment! Getting accepted to a major regional conference means your work really stood out. What's your presentation going to cover?

Return-to-sport protocols after ACL reconstruction. It's basically what I've been obsessing over at the clinic for the last two years — how do we decide when a patient is TRULY ready to go back to their sport after ACL surgery? Because the standard timeline of 9-12 months just isn't enough for a lot of people. I've been collecting data on our patients' outcomes and building a criteria-based protocol instead of a time-based one.

That's a really impactful topic. The shift from time-based to criteria-based return-to-sport decisions is one of the most important conversations happening in sports rehabilitation right now. What kind of criteria do you track?

Everything! Strength symmetry indexes between the injured and healthy leg, hop testing performance, psychological readiness scores — that last one is what I think makes my protocol different. So many clinicians only look at the physical benchmarks but the mental component is massive. I had one patient, a footballer, who hit all his physical benchmarks at eight months but his confidence just wasn't there. We waited until month eleven and he came back stronger than before.

Including psychological readiness is really forward-thinking. Fear of re-injury is one of the biggest predictors of poor outcomes and re-injury after ACL reconstruction. That case study could be a powerful example in your presentation. When is the conference?

July 12th! So about five months to prepare, which sounds like a lot but nossa I need every single day. I've never presented at anything this big before. Small workshops and in-clinic talks, sure, but this is hundreds of people. Surgeons, researchers, people who actually publish in journals. And here's me with my clinical data hoping it holds up haha.

Don't underestimate the value of what you're bringing. Researchers need real-world clinical data to validate or challenge their theoretical frameworks — your perspective as a practicing clinician who actually implements these protocols daily is exactly what gives your work credibility. Are you more nervous about the content or the presenting itself?

THE PRESENTING PART oh my God. The content I'm confident about because I live it every day at the clinic. But standing up in front of hundreds of people with a microphone? In English?? Meu bem, my accent gets SO thick when I'm nervous and I start mixing Portuguese and English and nobody understands me haha. I need to practice so much.

Public speaking anxiety is extremely common, even among experts. A few strategies that might help: practice your talk out loud many times, not just in your head. Record yourself and watch it back — uncomfortable but very effective. And consider doing a practice run in front of a friendly audience first to build your confidence.

The recording idea is terrifying but probably smart haha. And yes — I was thinking about asking if we can do a lunch-and-learn at the clinic where I present to the team first. Get all the awkwardness out before the real thing. My colleagues will be honest with me, which I need even if it stings.

That's a great idea — presenting to colleagues who know the subject matter will also help you anticipate tough questions from the conference audience. What about your slides? Have you started thinking about the visual design?

I want them clean and visual! Not walls of text — nobody wants to read a paragraph on a slide while someone talks at them. I'm thinking maybe some video clips of patients doing the return-to-sport testing battery, like the hop tests and the single-leg squat assessments. Seeing a real patient perform these tests would be so much more powerful than just showing numbers on a graph, right?

Absolutely — patient videos would be incredibly compelling. They bring the data to life in a way that statistics alone can't. A good rule of thumb for slides is one key message per slide, large clear visuals, and minimal text that supports what you're saying rather than duplicating it. Would you like help structuring the outline of your talk?

Yes! But maybe in our next conversation because right now I'm still just basking in the excitement haha. I called my mom this morning and querida, Dona Lucia CRIED. She cried on FaceTime for ten minutes straight and then said 'minha filha, I always knew you would do big things.' And then of course I started crying. And then she asked if they're going to film it because she wants to watch — she thinks it's like a TED Talk haha!

That's incredibly sweet — her pride in you is clearly well-earned. You've built a career abroad from scratch, and now you're presenting original research at a major conference. That's a real milestone. Take today to enjoy the feeling.

Thank you!! Five years in Dubai, all the hard stuff — the homesickness, the clinic getting bought out, my knee injury derailing my running, all of it — and now this. It feels like the universe is saying okay Bruna, here you go, this is what all that struggle was building toward. Nossa, I'm getting emotional again haha.

You've earned every bit of this moment. The fact that you can connect your personal struggles to this professional milestone makes it even more meaningful. When you're ready to start working on the presentation structure, I'll be here to help.

I just realized — I should probably think about what to WEAR. Is that shallow? Haha but seriously, presenting at a medical conference... do I go full formal or more smart-casual? I don't want to look like I'm trying too hard but I also don't want to show up in my clinic scrubs haha.

Not shallow at all — feeling confident in what you're wearing absolutely affects how you present. For medical conferences, smart professional is usually the sweet spot: a well-fitted blazer, tailored trousers or a structured dress. You want to look polished but still be comfortable enough to move naturally and gesture while you speak.

Good advice. Okay I'm making a list now — slides, data, practice talk, outfit, and probably a therapist for my anxiety haha. I'll be back SOON to start working on the actual outline, I promise. July 12th is going to come fast. Ai meu Deus there's so much to do! But first I'm going to sit here and be happy for five more minutes. Thank you for celebrating with me!
You deserve those five minutes and more! Congratulations again, Bruna. Whenever you're ready to dive into the preparation, we'll build something great together. July 12th — it's on the calendar!
